title: Krumkake
subtitle: Norwegian wafer-iron cone biscuits
excerpt: Thin, delicate Scandinavian wafers baked on a patterned iron and rolled into cones while still hot. The batter is a simple cardamom-scented egg batter; the skill is in the rolling before the wafer cools and crisps.
sourceNotes: Krumkake are a traditional Norwegian Christmas biscuit documented in Norwegian household manuals from the nineteenth century. The krumkake iron is a centuries-old tool in Scandinavian kitchens. Early recipes appear in Norwegian and Swedish domestic economy books from the 1860s-1880s. The cardamom flavouring is characteristic of Scandinavian baking.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keep in an airtight tin for up to 2 weeks. They soften quickly if left uncovered.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: Cooked on a krumkake iron or pizzelle maker on the hob or electric, not in an oven. Temperature refers to the iron heat: medium-high for even golden colour.

glossaryTerms.definition: A hinged cast-iron or electric iron with a patterned surface that imprints a traditional Scandinavian snowflake or rosette design onto the thin batter. An electric pizzelle maker works as a substitute.
Immediately after the wafer comes off the iron, it is pliable for about 10–15 seconds. It is quickly wrapped around a cone-shaped dowel or a rolled piece of cardboard, held until set, then slid off.

content.text: The batter for krumkake is mixed in minutes; the skill is in the speed of
is a specialist tool, but an electric pizzelle maker (the Italian wafer iron) works as a substitute. Have the cone mould (or a thick cardboard cone) ready before the batter goes onto the iron.

Whisk the eggs and sugar together until pale and slightly thickened, 2–3 minutes. Add the melted butter, then sift in the flour and cardamom and fold until smooth. Add the milk to bring the batter to a pourable consistency — it should flow like thick double cream. Rest for 5 minutes.

Heat the krumkake iron over a medium-high flame or set an electric model to medium-high. When a drop of water sizzles immediately on contact, it is ready. Lightly butter the iron plates. Place a heaped teaspoon of batter in the centre of the iron and press closed. Cook for 45–60 seconds per side on a stove-top model, or 60–90 seconds total on electric, until the wafer is pale golden and the pattern is set.

Open the iron carefully and lift the wafer immediately with a spatula. Within 10 seconds, wrap it around the cone mould and hold for 15 seconds until set. Slide the cone off carefully and cool on a rack. Repeat with the remaining batter, greasing the iron every 3–4 rounds. Store in an airtight tin; they lose their snap if exposed to moisture.
